tldr: Move from Prove's phone-centric US identity stack to Didit's document + biometric KYC API with a free tier and public pricing.
why_switch: Public, predictable pricing with a free tier vs. Prove's contact-sales model
Document scan, NFC chip read and selfie biometrics for global coverage, not just US carrier data
Less dependence on US phone-number signals for non-US users
Faster self-serve onboarding without enterprise procurement
Lower transparency lock-in (clearer plan tiers and exits)
what_to_export: Identity attributes you store server-side (name, DOB, address, document IDs)
Phone-possession and Pre-Fill audit logs you rely on for compliance
Risk/decision rules and thresholds documented in plain text
PII retention and deletion records for regulators
Any custom workflow configurations defined in the Prove dashboard
export_method: Use Prove's account-management or support channel to request a structured export of verification records and audit logs; in parallel, capture data you already store from webhook events into your own database. Treat your internal store as the system of record before cutting over. Consult Prove's official documentation and your contract for exact export endpoints and retention obligations.
import_method: Stand up Didit alongside Prove using its hosted verification flow and API. Run new sign-ups through Didit while letting existing users continue under Prove until re-verification is needed or naturally expires. Map your internal user IDs to Didit session IDs and store decision payloads from webhooks. Consult Didit's official docs for current endpoint paths and SDK setup.

concept_mapping.note: Different input model — collect identity from the document, not the carrier
Possession proof shifts from SIM to device + face
Compensate with device fingerprinting or a separate fraud signal vendor
Both expose status + decision; field names differ
Rebuild handlers against Didit's payload schema

gotchas: Phone-possession and SIM-swap signals do not have a direct equivalent — plan a compensating control
Re-verifying the entire user base is usually unnecessary; prefer forward-only cutover
Regulatory retention rules may force you to keep Prove records read-only for years
Webhook secrets, IP allowlists and signature schemes differ — re-test end-to-end
Free tier limits can change; validate volume against current published pricing
Confirm regional data residency matches your compliance posture before go-live

email.subject: Notice of non-renewal — Prove
email.body:
Hello Prove team,

We are migrating our identity verification stack to a different provider and will not be renewing our current Prove agreement at the end of the term.

Please confirm: (1) the effective end date and any wind-down steps, (2) the process to export our verification records and audit logs, and (3) data deletion timelines and certifications once the contract ends.

Thanks for the partnership.

Best regards,
